attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health check. She has no current symptoms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her mother died of ovarian cancer at age 45, and her sister was diagnosed with breast cancer at age 35. Her maternal aunt had ovarian cancer at age 50. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Breast examination reveals no masses or abnormalities.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a significant family history suggestive of hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome. The presence of early-onset breast and ovarian cancers in multiple first-degree relatives strongly suggests the possibility of a BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation.

Referral for genetic counselling is the most appropriate next step. The genetic counsellor can assess the family history in detail, calculate risk scores, discuss the implications of genetic testing, and arrange BRCA testing if appropriate. This information is crucial for making informed decisions about risk-reducing strategies.

Annual mammogram screening alone is not appropriate as the first step for this young woman. While enhanced screening may be recommended after risk assessment, mammography is less sensitive in young women with dense breast tissue, and screening typically starts at an earlier age in high-risk women.

Transvaginal ultrasound screening has not been shown to be effective for ovarian cancer screening, even in high-risk populations. Risk-reducing surgery might be discussed after genetic testing if a BRCA mutation is identified.

Oral contraceptive pill use can reduce ovarian cancer risk, but this should be discussed after genetic risk assessment, as hormonal contraception may have different risk-benefit profiles in BRCA mutation carriers.

Regular breast self-examination only is insufficient given the high-risk family history. While breast awareness is important, a comprehensive risk assessment and management strategy is required.

The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines recommend genetic testing if the combined BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation carrier probability is 10% or more. Risk assessment tools such as the Manchester score or IBIS model can help quantify this risk. If a BRCA mutation is identified, management options include enhanced surveillance, risk-reducing surgery, and chemoprevention.
learningPoints: Women with a strong family history of breast or ovarian cancer should be referred for genetic counselling and BRCA testing.
Risk assessment tools like IBIS or Manchester score help quantify breast and ovarian cancer risk.
First-degree relatives with early-onset breast or ovarian cancer significantly increase risk of BRCA mutations.
references: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Familial breast cancer: classification, care and managing breast cancer and related risks in people with a family history of breast cancer [Internet]. NICE guideline CG164. London: NICE; 2013 [updated 2023 Nov 14; cited 2024 Nov 29]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/cg164
Ramus SJ, Gayther SA. The contribution of BRCA1 and BRCA2 to ovarian cancer. Mol Oncol. 2009 Apr;3(2):138-50. doi: 10.1016/j.molonc.2009.02.001. Epub 2009 Feb 10. PMID: 19383375; PMCID: PMC5527889.
